topic: Theme 1: People and Lifestyle (Identity and Relationships; Healthy Living and Lifestyle; Education and Work)
subject: german

items.q: When addressing a stranger or someone older formally, which 'you' do you use?
items.correct: Sie (always capitalised, formal singular and plural).
items.why: Students default to du. German has THREE 'you' forms: du (informal singular), ihr (informal plural), Sie (formal singular OR plural - capitalised). Using du with a stranger or older person is rude. Sie takes third-person plural verb forms.

items.q: What is the difference between 'ich will' and 'ich möchte'?
items.correct: Ich will = I want (strong, direct); ich möchte = I would like (polite).
items.why: Students learn wollen as 'to want' and use it casually. 'Ich will...' sounds DEMANDING/childish. Polite German uses ICH MÖCHTE (Konjunktiv form of mögen) for 'I would like'. Essential for job/interview contexts.

A devastating faux ami. Bekommen looks like 'become' but means RECEIVE. 'Ich bekomme ein Geschenk' = I receive a present. To become = WERDEN. Confusion: 'I became a present' instead of 'I received a present'.

items.q: Which sentence correctly says 'I get on well with my mother'?
items.correct: Ich verstehe mich gut mit meiner Mutter.
items.misconception: Ich verstehe mich gut mit meine Mutter.
items.random_wrong: Ich verstehe mir gut mit meiner Mutter.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Ich verstehe mich gut mit meiner Mutter.. “Ich verstehe mich gut mit meine Mutter.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which form of 'you' do you use addressing a German teacher?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Sie. “du” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many grammatical cases does standard German have?
items.why: Exact answer: 4 cases. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
